result.markdown: --- title: "Normalization & Feature Selection" subtitle: "Correct for sequencing depth and identify highly variable genes" categories: [scRNA-seq, Seurat, Normalization] --- scRNA-seq · Chapter 02 ## Overview Raw UMI counts are confounded by differences in sequencing depth across cells. Normalization removes this technical variation, allowing meaningful comparison of gene expression levels. Feature selection then focuses downstream analysis on genes that carry the most biological signal. **This chapter covers:** 1. Log-normalization (classical approach) 2. SCTransform v2 (variance-stabilizing approach) 3. Highly variable gene (HVG) selection 4. Data scaling --- ## Setup ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(Seurat) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} seu <- readRDS("data/processed/seu_01_filtered.rds") ``` ::: --- ## Approach 1: Log-Normalization The standard approach: normalize each cell to 10,000 counts (CPM-like), then apply a log1p transformation. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} seu <- NormalizeData( seu, normalization.method = "LogNormalize", scale.factor = 10000 ) ``` ::: --- ## Approach 2: SCTransform v2 (Recommended) SCTransform uses regularized negative binomial regression to model the mean-variance relationship, producing Pearson residuals that are largely independent of sequencing depth. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} seu <- SCTransform( seu, vst.flavor = "v2", # use SCTransform v2 vars.to.regress = "pct_mt", # optionally regress out mt percentage variable.features.n = 3000, verbose = FALSE ) ``` ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Which normalization should I use? - **LogNormalize** → faster, well-understood, compatible with all downstream Seurat functions - **SCTransform v2** → generally better for datasets with high sequencing depth variance; required when integrating with `SCTransform`-normalized reference datasets For most published workflows, SCTransform v2 is now the default recommendation. ::: --- ## Highly Variable Gene Selection HVGs are genes that vary more across cells than expected by chance — they capture biologically meaningful variation. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # If using LogNormalize (skip if using SCTransform — it selects HVGs internally) seu <- FindVariableFeatures( seu, selection.method = "vst", nfeatures = 3000 ) # Visualise top HVGs top20 <- head(VariableFeatures(seu), 20) p <- VariableFeaturePlot(seu) LabelPoints(plot = p, points = top20, repel = TRUE, xnudge = 0, ynudge = 0) ``` ::: --- ## Scaling Scaling centers each gene to mean 0 and variance 1 across cells, preventing highly expressed genes from dominating PCA. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Scale only HVGs (faster; sufficient for PCA/UMAP) seu <- ScaleData(seu, features = VariableFeatures(seu)) # --- OR scale all genes (required for heatmaps showing non-HVG genes) --- seu <- ScaleData(seu, features = rownames(seu)) ``` ::: ::: {.callout-note} When using SCTransform, scaling is performed internally. Calling `ScaleData()` again is not necessary for standard workflows. ::: --- ## Save Checkpoint ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_02_normalized.rds") ``` ::: --- ## Session Info ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sessionInfo() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31) Platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu Running under: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS
